title: Ôn thi TN THPT Thành phố Huế - Đề số 3

part1.q:
Read the passage and mark the letter A, B, C or D on your answer sheet to indicate the best answer to each of the following questions from 1 to 8.
Obsessive-compulsive disorder (OCD) is a mental health condition classified as an anxiety disorder, affecting around 2–4% of people. Individuals with OCD experience persistent, unwanted thoughts known as obsessions, which often lead to repetitive behaviors called compulsions. Although sufferers usually recognize that their thoughts are irrational, they still feel unable to control them.
Obsessions create intense anxiety and may focus on issues such as contamination, order, or doubt. For example, a person may constantly worry about germs or feel uneasy if objects are not arranged symmetrically. Others may repeatedly question whether they have locked a door or turned off an appliance. In some cases, intrusive thoughts may involve inappropriate or disturbing impulses.
To relieve anxiety, individuals with OCD perform compulsive actions that may be physical, such as excessive checking, or arranging objects, or mental, such as counting or silently repeating phrases. While these rituals may temporarily reduce stress, they often interfere with daily life. Over time, people with OCD may develop strict rules for their behaviors. For instance, someone may feel the need to wash their hands a certain number of times before eating. Interestingly, compulsions are not always logically connected to the obsession. A person disturbed by violent images may cope by counting repeatedly or touching an object in a fixed pattern.
Many individuals delay seeking treatment due to embarrassment or fear of being judged. However, without proper support, OCD can significantly affect careers, relationships, and overall well-being. Early diagnosis and appropriate treatment can help sufferers manage their symptoms and improve their quality of life.
(Adapted from Barron's IELTS: International English Language Testing System)
The word persistent in paragraph 1 mostly means ______.

Which of the following is TRUE according to paragraph 1?
options: Only 2% of the global population is affected by OCD.
Many people with OCD can keep their thoughts under control.
OCD sufferers are unaware their thoughts are irrational.
OCD is a mental health condition related to anxiety.

Which of the following best paraphrases the underlined sentence in paragraph 3?
options: Compulsions can eliminate anxiety completely and may improve daily routines.
Compulsions may reduce stress briefly but can disrupt normal activities.
Compulsions, which may temporarily reduce stress, can rarely affect daily life.
Compulsions can increase stress and may have no obvious benefits.

Read the following leaflet and mark the letter A, B, C or D on your answer sheet to indicate the option that best fits each of the numbered blanks from 9 to 14.
How to live a healthy lifestyle
Living a healthy lifestyle is important for your health and happiness. (9) ______, many people do not pay enough attention to simple habits like eating well and staying active. These habits can help prevent diseases, (10) ______ mental health and increase life expectancy.
Here are some simple ways to stay healthy:
 Eat a balanced diet and avoid too (11) ______ processed food.
 Do (12) ______ for at least 30 minutes every day to stay healthy.
 Get enough sleep so your body can rest and recover.
 Control stress to avoid (13) ______ effects on your health.
Build good daily habits to achieve the best (14) ______ from your efforts.
(Adapted from https://www.healthline.com/health/fitness-nutrition/healthy-lifestyle-benefits)

Mark the letter A, B, C or D on your answer sheet to indicate the best arrangement of utterances or sentences to make a cohesive and coherent exchange or text in each of the following questions from 15 to 19.
a. As a result, I can clarify what kind of career I truly want to pursue now.
b. At first, I felt completely confused because there were so many career options available to me.
c. To address this, I decided to attend several career workshops and talk to people working in different fields.
d. Choosing a suitable career is not an easy decision, especially for young people today.
e. During these activities, I gradually realised that my interests and abilities play a crucial role in making the right choice.

Mark the letter A, B, C or D on your answer sheet to indicate the best arrangement of utterances or sentences to make a cohesive and coherent exchange or text in each of the following questions from 15 to 19.
a. Minh: That’s a fair point, but if we don't meet in person at least once, it’ll be much harder to brainstorm the creative parts of this presentation.
b. Lan: I see what you mean, but I think it’s more convenient to just share a Google Doc and work on our assigned sections individually.
c. Minh: It’s a bit worrying that our group project is due next Friday and we still haven't decided how we’re going to collaborate.
d. Lan: Fair enough. In that case, let’s have a quick coffee meeting this Saturday to finalize the main ideas, and then we can finish the rest online.
e. Minh: Exactly! That way, we get the best of both worlds, effective brainstorming and the flexibility to work from home.

Mark the letter A, B, C or D on your answer sheet to indicate the best arrangement of utterances or sentences to make a cohesive and coherent exchange or text in each of the following questions from 15 to 19.
a. However, when poorly managed, it may result in environmental degradation, social inequality, and the commercialization of local identities.
b. Tourism has long been recognized as a powerful driver of economic growth.
c. Responsible tourism can also foster cultural preservation by motivating communities to maintain their traditions and historical landmarks.
d. The challenge lies in promoting sustainable tourism that maximizes socio-economic benefits while safeguarding both cultural integrity and ecological balance.
e. It contributes significantly to national income through employment creation and the stimulation of related industries such as transport and hospitality.

Mark the letter A, B, C or D on your answer sheet to indicate the best arrangement of utterances or sentences to make a cohesive and coherent exchange or text in each of the following questions from 15 to 19.
a. Bill: That’s very kind of you! I'll try it right now to see if it works.
b. Bill: I'm struggling to print this report; could you show me how to turn on the machine?
c. Rose: Of course, just press the large power button on the right side and wait for the green light.

Mark the letter A, B, C or D on your answer sheet to indicate the best arrangement of utterances or sentences to make a cohesive and coherent exchange or text in each of the following questions from 15 to 19.
Dear Ms. Linda,
a. Despite our prior agreements regarding the schedule, the main courses were served nearly an hour late, leaving many of our guests with lukewarm meals.
b. I am writing to formally express my dissatisfaction with the catering service provided by your agency at our annual gala last Saturday.
c. I look forward to receiving your proposal on how you intend to rectify this situation and prevent such lapses in the future.
d. Given your company’s standing in the industry, I found this lack of professional standards and attention to detail particularly disappointing.
e. Consequently, I would like to request a substantial discount on the final invoice as compensation for the disruption to our event.
Yours sincerely,
James Bennett

Read the passage and mark the letter A, B, C or D on your answer sheet to indicate the best answer to each of the following questions from 20 to 29.
For centuries, human labor was assessed through productivity - the clear output of physical or intellectual effort. Yet in the twenty-first century, automation is transforming this idea. Machines not only perform repetitive tasks with accuracy but also increasingly engage in roles once thought to require uniquely human cognitive sophistication. The question, therefore, is not whether automation will replace work, but how societies will redefine human value in an automated world.
At first glance, automation appears to promise liberation. I Freed from monotonous labor, individuals could, in theory, devote themselves to leisure, which was once reserved for the privileged. Yet, without effective policies, the current transformation may increase inequality, creating a divided world where a small elite controls data infrastructures while others compete for precarious digital tasks.
Economists disagree about whether automation truly destroys jobs or merely reshapes them. II Some argue that new forms of employment will emerge while others warn that the pace of change outstrips humanity’s ability to adapt. Unlike past industrial shifts, where retraining could occur over generations, the digital transition unfolds in decades or even years. This rapid change may leave many people economically vulnerable and unsure about their future. More importantly, as traditional roles disappear, individuals may begin to lose their sense of identity and purpose.
Education systems are also under pressure from automation. III Traditional models, which prepare students for stable careers, are becoming less relevant. The competencies most valued now are adaptability, critical thinking, and emotional intelligence which cannot be easily taught through rote learning. If schools continue to prioritize memorization over inquiry, they risk producing graduates adept at passing exams but ill-equipped for fluid, interdisciplinary futures. Consequently, education systems worldwide must reimagine learning as a lifelong process rather than a finite preparation for employment.
Moreover, automation compels us to reconsider the essence of intelligence itself. IV If intelligence can be imitated without awareness, society must decide whether efficiency should always triumph over authenticity. The debate, in the end, is less about economics than about ethics: what kind of civilization do we wish to become when productivity is no longer our defining virtue?
Ultimately, the future of work may depend not on resisting automation but on developing new forms of cooperation between humans and machines. The future of work, like the future of humanity, will be shaped not by what machines can do, but by what we choose to value.
(Adapted from MIT Technology Review)
According to paragraph 1, automation _______.
options: leads to the replacement of human labor in all creative roles
remains confined entirely to simple and repetitive routine tasks
results in the complete removal of human judgement in most fields
allows machines to perform tasks requiring human cognitive abilities

Which of the following best summarizes paragraph 2?
options: Automation will liberate everyone from labor, giving them chances to pursue leisure.
Technological progress guarantees that job opportunities are distributed fairly across society.
While automation may offer freedom, it risks increasing inequality and social division.
The control of data infrastructures by a small elite is the only way to ensure economic growth

Ultimately, the future of work may depend not on resisting automation but on developing new forms of cooperation between humans and machines. The future of work, like the future of humanity, will be shaped not by what machines can do, but by what we choose to value.
(Adapted from MIT Technology Review)
Which of the following best paraphrases the underlined sentence in paragraph 3?
options: In the past, people had much less time to learn new skills during industrial changes than they do today.
Compared to earlier industrial transformations, the digital transition is occurring at a much faster pace.
Historical industrial revolutions lasted much shorter than the modern digital transition we see now.
Modern technology allows workers to retrain themselves more frequently than past generations could.

part1.q:
Read the passage and mark the letter A, B, C or D on your answer sheet to indicate the best answer to each of the following questions from 20 to 29.
For centuries, human labor was assessed through productivity - the clear output of physical or intellectual effort. Yet in the twenty-first century, automation is transforming this idea. Machines not only perform repetitive tasks with accuracy but also increasingly engage in roles once thought to require uniquely human cognitive sophistication. The question, therefore, is not whether automation will replace work, but how societies will redefine human value in an automated world.
At first glance, automation appears to promise liberation. I Freed from monotonous labor, individuals could, in theory, devote themselves to leisure, which was once reserved for the privileged. Yet, without effective policies, the current transformation may increase inequality, creating a divided world where a small elite controls data infrastructures while others compete for precarious digital tasks.
Economists disagree about whether automation truly destroys jobs or merely reshapes them. II Some argue that new forms of employment will emerge while others warn that the pace of change outstrips humanity’s ability to adapt. Unlike past industrial shifts, where retraining could occur over generations, the digital transition unfolds in decades or even years. This rapid change may leave many people economically vulnerable and unsure about their future. More importantly, as traditional roles disappear, individuals may begin to lose their sense of identity and purpose.
Education systems are also under pressure from automation. III Traditional models, which prepare students for stable careers, are becoming less relevant. The competencies most valued now are adaptability, critical thinking, and emotional intelligence which cannot be easily taught through rote learning. If schools continue to prioritize memorization over inquiry, they risk producing graduates adept at passing exams but ill-equipped for fluid, interdisciplinary futures. Consequently, education systems worldwide must reimagine learning as a lifelong process rather than a finite preparation for employment.
Moreover, automation compels us to reconsider the essence of intelligence itself. IV If intelligence can be imitated without awareness, society must decide whether efficiency should always triumph over authenticity. The debate, in the end, is less about economics than about ethics: what kind of civilization do we wish to become when productivity is no longer our defining virtue?
Ultimately, the future of work may depend not on resisting automation but on developing new forms of cooperation between humans and machines. The future of work, like the future of humanity, will be shaped not by what machines can do, but by what we choose to value.
(Adapted from MIT Technology Review)
In paragraph 3, what does the author suggest is a major risk of automation?
options: the disappearance of all job opportunities
the loss of human identity and purpose

The author criticizes education systems mainly for _______.
options: failing to prepare students for unpredictable futures

part1.q:
Read the following piece of news and mark the letter A, B, C or D on your answer sheet to indicate the option that best fits each of the numbered blanks from 30 to 35.
London’s Clean Air Plan Causes Public Debate
The Greater London Authority has expanded the Ultra Low Emission Zone (ULEZ) to cover all parts of London, a policy that has received strong backing from Mayor Sadiq Khan. Under this scheme, drivers who use older and more polluting vehicles are required to pay a daily (30) ______ in order to enter the zone. The purpose of this policy is to reduce air pollution and improve public health across the city.
City officials argue that air quality has already improved (31) ______ since the policy was introduced. However, many residents have expressed concern about the financial impact, especially those living in outer areas, where people often do not have easy access to newer vehicles. As a result, some individuals report (32) ______ more money because they are forced to upgrade their cars sooner than expected.
Environmental groups strongly support the policy, saying that it is necessary to tackle (33) ______ environmental problems. Transport for London has introduced financial assistance to help drivers adapt. Still, some people question (34) ______ specific impacts the policy has on different groups, and how the government will (35) ______ these concerns effectively.

Read the following passage and mark the letter A, B, C or D on your answer sheet to indicate the option that best fits each of the numbered blanks from 36 to 40.
Music has always reflected the spirit of its time, yet never before has it been so deeply shaped by technology. The shift from physical albums to streaming platforms has transformed how people access, share, and value sound. While listeners now enjoy unlimited choice, artists often face lower income and unclear ownership rights, (36) _______.
In this new ecosystem, success is no longer measured solely by sales but by visibility - the number of clicks, followers, or viral moments. (37) _______. However, critics warn that such algorithms reward repetition and predictability, discouraging experimentation that once drove musical innovation.
For musicians, the challenge is to maintain artistic integrity while adapting to rapid digital change. Many turn to live performances, crowdfunding, or niche communities to regain autonomy. (38) _______. These efforts reflect a wider attempt to reconnect the emotional power of music with genuine human experience.
At the same time, listeners are also changing. With personalized playlists and recommendation engines, (39) _______. Consequently, music becomes a background habit rather than a focused art form. The result is a paradox: music is more accessible than ever, yet often less meaningful as a shared cultural experience. Still, despite its upheavals, (40) _______. What endures is the human desire to create and connect through sound.
(Adapted from BBC Culture and The Guardian Music)
options: leaving many artists questioning whether creativity can survive commercial algorithms
many artists who question the survival of creativity because of commercial algorithms
will be the fact that creativity survives commercial algorithms, which is unquestionable
but the question as to whether artists’ creativity or commercial algorithms will survive.

options: Likewise, popularity has recently been indicated by musical success

Anyway, it is the indication of both popularity and musical success
